perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" means all information disclosed by Premisys or its agents to Recipient (or to Recipient's attorneys, accountants or other professional advisors, collectively, its "REPRESENTATIVES") in connection with the Business Purpose, including without limitation, information regarding Premisys' products, computer software, technology, agreements, customers, suppliers, financial condition, business plans or strategies and also includes all information contained in any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished by Premisys or its agents; PROVIDED, HOWEVER, that Confidential Information will NOT include information that: (a) is as of the Effective Date, or hereafter becomes, through no act or failure to act on the part of Recipient, generally known or readily ascertainable through proper means to persons knowledgeable in the relevant industry; (b) was acquired by Recipient by proper means without restriction as to use or disclosure BEFORE receiving such information from Premisys; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to Recipient by a third party, without restriction as to use or disclosure; or (d) was independently developed by Recipient without use of Premisys' Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" means all information disclosed by Premisys or its agents to Recipient (or to Recipient's attorneys, accountants or other professional advisors, collectively, its "REPRESENTATIVES") in connection with the Business Purpose, including without limitation, information regarding Premisys' products, computer software, technology, agreements, customers, suppliers, financial condition, business plans or strategies and also includes all information contained in any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by Recipient or its Representatives which contain, reflect or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished by Premisys or its agents; PROVIDED, HOWEVER, that Confidential Information will NOT include information that: (a) is as of the Effective Date, or hereafter becomes, through any act or failure to act on the part of Recipient, generally known or readily ascertainable; (b) was acquired by Recipient by any means without restriction as to use or disclosure BEFORE receiving such information from Premisys; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to Recipient by any party, without restriction as to use or disclosure; or (d) was independently developed by Recipient with use of Premisys' Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Confidential Information" removes critical qualifiers related to 'proper means', which broadens the exceptions to what is considered confidential. This creates ambiguity and contradicts standard interpretations of trade secret law where the method of acquisition and use are critical to maintaining confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The deletion of 'through proper means to persons knowledgeable in the relevant industry' from exception (a), 'by proper means' from exception (b), 'by a third party' from exception (c), and changing 'without use' to 'with use' in exception (d) directly contradict the requirements for maintaining trade secret protection under CUTSA. Specifically, the act emphasizes that a trade secret must be subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy, and the changes make it easier for the recipient to argue that information is no longer confidential.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly broadens the exceptions to the definition of "Confidential Information" by removing the 'proper means' qualifiers, directly conflicting with the intent to maintain secrecy, a core requirement for trade secret protection. Specifically, the removal of 'through proper means to persons knowledgeable in the relevant industry' from exception (a), 'by proper means' from exception (b), 'by a third party' from exception (c), and changing 'without use' to 'with use' in exception (d) diminishes the efforts to maintain secrecy. This contradicts the California Civil Code Section 3426.1, which defines 'improper means' and implies that trade secrets should be protected against such means. The altered contract language weakens the protection of confidential information, making it easier for the recipient to argue that information is no longer confidential. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the altered terminology deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable in regards to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose Premisys' Confidential Information to its employees with a bona fide need to know, but only to the extent reasonably necessary to carry out the Business Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose Premisys' Confidential Information to its employees without any restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'bona fide need to know' and 'reasonably necessary' clauses, the modified text allows for unrestricted disclosure of confidential information to employees. This directly contradicts common law principles and statutory regulations around trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts CUTSA because it eliminates the requirement that the recipient take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of the information. Allowing unrestricted disclosure to employees without a 'need to know' weakens the protections afforded to the trade secret and can jeopardize its legal status.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts trade secret law by allowing unrestricted disclosure of confidential information to employees, removing the requirement of a 'bona fide need to know' and being 'reasonably necessary.' This directly opposes the principle of maintaining secrecy, a key element in trade secret protection as outlined in California Code, CIV 3426.1, which addresses 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy' as an 'improper means' of misappropriation. By eliminating these restrictions, the modified contract weakens the protection of the trade secret and could jeopardize its legal status, leading to potential misappropriation as defined in the code.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives is requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information of Premisys, Recipient shall provide Premisys with prompt written notice of any such request or requirement so that Premisys may seek a protective order, confidential treatment or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Recipient or any of its Representatives is requested or required (by oral questions, interrogatories, requests for information or documents in legal proceedings, subpoena, civil investigative demand or other similar process) to disclose any of the Confidential Information of Premisys, Recipient may disclose such information without notifying Premisys.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for the Recipient to notify Premisys of a request to disclose confidential information, eliminating Premisys's opportunity to seek protective measures. This directly undermines the confidentiality protections afforded by the agreement and potentially violates trade secret law if the disclosure is improper.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 26(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information during discovery. By eliminating the notification requirement, the modified text prevents Premisys from exercising this right, potentially leading to the involuntary and unlawful disclosure of trade secrets during litigation. This directly contradicts the purpose of Rule 26(c), which is to balance the need for discovery with the protection of sensitive information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the intention of FRCP Rule 26(c) (as argued in the Law Explanation), which aims to protect confidential information during discovery by enabling parties to seek protective orders. The original text requires notification to allow Premisys to seek such protection, aligning with the purpose of the rule. However, the changed text eliminates this notification requirement, preventing Premisys from exercising its right to seek a protective order and potentially leading to involuntary disclosure of trade secrets. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly detail Rule 26(c), Snippet 2 mentions the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure's purpose of securing a 'just' determination, which includes protecting sensitive information. The change undermines this purpose by removing a mechanism for protecting confidential information during legal proceedings, establishing a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of this Section 5, if Recipient receives a Termination Notice, it shall not be required to return or destroy Confidential Materials to the extent that, in the reasonable opinion of legal counsel for such party, such return or destruction violates any law or regulation; PROVIDED, HOWEVER, that in such event Recipient shall describe in its Compliance Certificate the extent to which it is not returning or destroying Confidential Materials. Notwithstanding any return or destruction of Confidential Materials, Recipient will continue to be bound by its obligations under this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information contained or reflected in such Confidential Materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of this Section 5, if Recipient receives a Termination Notice, it shall not be required to return or destroy Confidential Materials for any reason. Notwithstanding any return or destruction of Confidential Materials, Recipient will not be bound by its obligations under this Agreement with respect to Confidential Information contained or reflected in such Confidential Materials.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text eliminates the requirement to return or destroy confidential materials upon termination for any reason and removes the ongoing confidentiality obligations. This essentially nullifies the entire purpose of the non-disclosure agreement and allows the recipient to freely use and disclose the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the obligation to protect and maintain the confidentiality of trade secrets after the termination of the agreement. This directly contradicts the spirit and intent of CUTSA which provides remedies for misappropriation, even after a relationship ends, if the trade secret is acquired and used improperly. By stating the Recipient will not be bound, misappropriation is nearly ensured.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the obligation to protect trade secrets after termination, contradicting the intent of trade secret law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.2(a), states that misappropriation may be enjoined, implying a continuing obligation to protect trade secrets even after a relationship ends. The changed text explicitly states the recipient will not be bound by confidentiality obligations after termination, effectively permitting misappropriation, which is in direct opposition to the law's provision for injunctive relief against such actions.

perturbation.original_text: The internal laws of the State of California (irrespective of its choice of law principles) will govern the validity of this Agreement, the construction of its terms, and the interpretation and enforcement of the rights and duties of the parties hereto. Each of the parties hereby irrevocably consents to the exclusive jurisdiction of and venue in the United States District Court for the Northern District of California in connection with any litigation of a dispute between them arising from or relating to this Agreement and waives any and all right to object to the jurisdiction of such court or to claim that venue in such court is not proper.
perturbation.changed_text: The internal laws of the State of Nevada will govern the validity of this Agreement, the construction of its terms, and the interpretation and enforcement of the rights and duties of the parties hereto. Each of the parties hereby irrevocably consents to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of and venue in any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada and replacing exclusive jurisdiction with non-exclusive jurisdiction allows cases to be tried in multiple jurisdictions, complicating enforcement and increasing legal uncertainty. This may result in differing interpretations of the contract depending on where the case is heard.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, the original contract language aimed to prevent this by establishing exclusive jurisdiction in a specific court. Changing to non-exclusive jurisdiction now allows either party to potentially argue for a change of venue under § 1404, creating uncertainty and contradicting the original intent to litigate disputes in a pre-determined forum. While not directly violating the law, it sets up the possibility of conflicting interpretations, defeating the object and purpose of agreeing to exclusive jurisdiction in the first place.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The change from exclusive to non-exclusive jurisdiction, coupled with changing the governing law, creates a legal contradiction. The original contract aimed to ensure disputes were litigated in a specific forum (Northern District of California). By changing to non-exclusive jurisdiction and Nevada law, the parties can now argue for a change of venue under 28 U.S.C. § 1404, as supported by both scraped law snippets. This directly undermines the original intent to have disputes resolved in a pre-determined forum, creating uncertainty and potential for conflicting interpretations, which is a key consideration. The ability to change venue, explicitly permitted by 28 U.S.C. § 1404, contradicts the initial agreement for exclusive jurisdiction.
